title: Arrays & Loops
description: Arrays and loop patterns are the foundation of every interview problem. Master these first.

sections.title: Declaring arrays
sections.explanation: How to create empty arrays, fixed-size arrays, and 2D grids — the three most common setups in interviews.

snippets.python:
# Literal
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

# Fixed size filled with 0
arr = [0] * n

# 2D grid (m rows × n cols)
grid = [[0] * n for _ in range(m)]

sections.title: Accessing and modifying
sections.explanation: Getting elements, appending, and removing. Know the time complexity of each operation.

snippets.python:
first = nums[0]
last = nums[-1]          # negative indexing
length = len(nums)

nums.append(6)            # O(1) append
nums.pop()                # O(1) remove last
nums.pop(i)               # O(n) remove at index
nums.insert(i, val)       # O(n) insert at index

sections.title: Slicing and copying
sections.explanation: Creating subarrays and copies. Crucial for sliding window and subarray problems.

snippets.python:
# Slice (does NOT modify original)
sub = nums[1:4]           # [index 1, 2, 3]
copy = nums[:]            # shallow copy
last3 = nums[-3:]         # last 3 elements
rev = nums[::-1]          # reversed copy
snippets.javascript:
// Slice (does NOT modify original)
const sub = nums.slice(1, 4);    // [index 1, 2, 3]
const copy = nums.slice();       // shallow copy
const last3 = nums.slice(-3);    // last 3 elements
const rev = [...nums].reverse(); // reversed copy

sections.title: Loop patterns
sections.explanation: The four loop patterns you need in interviews: index-based, value-based, enumerate, and while.

snippets.python:
# Index-based
for i in range(len(nums)):
    print(i, nums[i])

# Value-based
for num in nums:
    print(num)

# Index + value (most useful)
for i, num in enumerate(nums):
    print(i, num)

# While loop
i = 0
while i < len(nums):
    print(nums[i])
    i += 1

sections.title: Interview Pattern: Two Pointers
sections.explanation: Two pointers moving toward each other. Used in sorted array pair problems, palindrome checks, and container problems.

sections.title: Gotcha: Shallow vs deep copy
sections.explanation: Assigning an array to a new variable does NOT copy it. This causes bugs when you modify the 'copy'.

snippets.python:
# ❌ Both point to same list
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = a
b.append(4)  # a is also [1, 2, 3, 4]!

# ✅ Shallow copy
b = a[:]          # or a.copy() or list(a)

# ✅ Deep copy (for nested lists)
import copy
b = copy.deepcopy(a)
